definition: An entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. Under GDPR Article 4(7), the controller decides why and how personal data is processed and bears primary responsibility for compliance.

An entity that processes personal data on behalf of a data controller. Processors act under the controller's instructions and must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure data security.

An identified or identifiable natural person whose personal data is being collected, held, or processed. Data subjects have specific rights under privacy laws including access, rectification, erasure, and portability.

Any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. This includes names, identification numbers, location data, online identifiers, and factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural, or social identity of a person.

The European Union's comprehensive data protection regulation that took effect on May 25, 2018. It harmonizes data protection laws across the EU/EEA, establishes data subject rights, and imposes obligations on organizations processing personal data with penalties up to €20M or 4% of global revenue.

California's landmark consumer privacy law effective January 1, 2020, granting residents rights to know, delete, and opt-out of the sale of their personal information. Applies to businesses meeting revenue, data volume, or data sale thresholds.

An amendment and expansion of the CCPA, effective January 1, 2023, that created the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA), added rights to correct and limit use of sensitive personal information, and introduced automated decision-making provisions.
source: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 et seq. (as amended)

term: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD)
definition: Brazil's General Data Protection Law, effective September 2020, modeled on the GDPR. Establishes data subject rights, legal bases for processing, and created the ANPD (National Data Protection Authority) as the enforcement body.

Singapore's comprehensive data protection law governing the collection, use, and disclosure of personal data by private organizations. Administered by the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) with enforcement powers.

The European Union's regulation on artificial intelligence, adopted in 2024, establishing a risk-based framework for AI systems. Prohibits certain AI practices, imposes strict requirements on high-risk AI, and mandates transparency for AI systems interacting with people.

A process required under GDPR Article 35 when data processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. DPIAs must describe processing operations, assess necessity and proportionality, and identify measures to mitigate risks.

Documentation required under GDPR Article 30 that controllers and processors must maintain describing their data processing activities. Must include purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and security measures.

One of six legal bases for processing personal data under GDPR Article 6(1)(f). Processing is lawful when necessary for the legitimate interests of the controller or a third party, provided those interests are not overridden by the data subject's rights and freedoms.

A legal basis for processing personal data requiring a freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous indication of the data subject's agreement. Under GDPR, consent must be as easy to withdraw as to give, and controllers must demonstrate valid consent was obtained.

Categories of personal data that are particularly sensitive and receive enhanced protection under GDPR Article 9. Includes data revealing racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, genetic data, biometric data, health data, and data concerning sex life or sexual orientation.

A request made by a data subject to a controller to obtain confirmation of whether their personal data is being processed, access to that data, and supplementary information about the processing. Controllers must respond within one month under GDPR.

term: Right to Erasure (Right to Be Forgotten)
definition: The right of data subjects to obtain from the controller the erasure of their personal data without undue delay in certain circumstances, including when data is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, or processing is unlawful.

The right of data subjects to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format, and to transmit that data to another controller without hindrance. Applies when processing is based on consent or contract and carried out by automated means.

The data protection authority of the EU member state where a controller or processor has its main establishment. Under the GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism, the lead authority coordinates cross-border cases with other concerned supervisory authorities.

The GDPR cooperation mechanism ensuring that organizations operating across multiple EU member states deal primarily with one lead supervisory authority rather than every national DPA. The lead authority coordinates with other concerned authorities before issuing final decisions.

An independent public authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing data protection laws within its jurisdiction. Each EU/EEA member state has at least one DPA, and they cooperate through the EDPB on cross-border matters.

Pre-approved contractual terms adopted by the European Commission that provide appropriate safeguards for international transfers of personal data from the EU/EEA to third countries. Updated SCCs were adopted in June 2021 with a modular approach covering four transfer scenarios.

A determination by the European Commission that a third country or territory provides an adequate level of data protection comparable to the EU. Personal data can flow freely to adequate jurisdictions without additional safeguards. Examples include the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.

The transmission or making available of personal data to a recipient in a third country or international organization outside the EU/EEA. Transfers require adequate safeguards such as adequacy decisions, SCCs, BCRs, or derogations under Article 49.
